text: The robotic view shows the surgeon performing lymph node dissection, which is typically done after the prostatectomy and before the anastomosis, but was completed earlier in this case.

The surgeon displays the node packets that have been removed from both sides, showing the external iliac artery, vein, and obturator nerve. The bladder remains elevated to expose the seminal vesicles (SVs). The node packets are visible and will be bagged later.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection to access the seminal vesicles (SVs), demonstrating a technique that may differ from other approaches.

The surgeon begins the posterior dissection by identifying the line where the yellow perirectal fat meets the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder, approximately one centimeter above this junction.

The surgeon makes an incision approximately one centimeter above the junction of the yellow perirectal fat and the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder. The dissection proceeds through a thin layer of tissue, aiming to reach the Denonvilliers' fascia. The vas deferens is identified first, lifted, revealing the seminal vesicles below.

The surgeon lifts the left seminal vesicle (SV) and works on the downside, following the medial aspect around the corner to the tip. Then, the SV is pulled down into the contralateral shoulder, revealing the lateral aspect for further dissection.
The surgeon makes a narrow incision in the midline during the dissection, ensuring that the ureters remain out of the surgical field and are not at risk of injury.

The surgeon avoids lateral dissection of the vas deferens to prevent encountering the ureter, instead making an incision one centimeter above the line where yellow tissue meets white tissue, proceeding down to the Denonvilliers' fascia.

The surgeon identifies the Denonvilliers' fascia, ensuring dissection remains above this layer to avoid rectal injury. The surgical field shows careful dissection along the adventitia of the seminal vesicles or vas deferens, maintaining proximity to these structures to prevent ureteral injury and minimize bleeding.

The surgeon carefully retracts tissue to maintain visibility while dissecting around the seminal vesicles (SVs). The retraction is adjusted to accommodate the varying size of the SVs, ensuring the surgical field remains open and visible.
The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope throughout the prostatectomy procedure. The bladder is visibly dropped due to the lift-nose dissection technique, which affects the posterior dissection process.
The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia, revealing the contour of the prostate gland, which aids in identifying the bladder neck during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia before addressing the bladder neck. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) is cut and oversewn during the apical dissection at the end of the prostatectomy, rather than pre-placing a stitch.
The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) cold during the prostatectomy, observing that the bleeding is manageable despite the pneumoperitoneum pressure being set at six.

The surgeon uses a low cautery setting to carefully dissect through the bladder neck, revealing distinct muscle fibers. The cautery is applied gently, allowing the muscle fibers to contract away, ensuring clear visualization and avoiding excessive charring.
The surgeon maintains a flat dissection plane, moving back and forth to approach the urethra without creating a deep hole, ensuring precise navigation to avoid entering the prostate inadvertently.
The surgeon encounters venous bleeding, indicating proximity to the urethra during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the prostatic urethra, noting venous bleeding from the mucosal tissue. The Foley catheter is visible, with a black line drawn at the level of the eye hole to aid in proper positioning and rotation.

The surgeon uses a marking pen and continues the dissection with a light touch of the cautery, minimizing charring while following the bladder neck fibers. The left hand is used for traction to aid in the bladder neck sparing technique.

The surgeon performs a figure-of-eight stitch at the three and nine o'clock positions on the bladder neck, focusing on freeing the corners of the posterior bladder neck to maintain tightness. The prostate is observed to have a small median lobe, with the MRI indicating a size of approximately sixty grams.

The surgeon frees the corners of the bladder neck from the prostate, ensuring the bladder neck remains tight. The left hand is used to provide tension and exposure, facilitating precise cutting once the corners are brought towards the surgeon.

The surgeon uses the left hand to provide traction on the bladder neck, stretching the muscle fibers to identify the correct surgical plane. The cautery is applied with minimal char, causing visible contraction of the muscle fibers. The prostate tissue appears denser and marshmallow-like compared to the bladder neck fibers, indicating the correct anatomical location.

The surgeon is working within the rectal plane, opting to leave the Denonvilliers' fascia on the prostate and dissecting into the perirectal fat plane. This approach is described as more natural and less sticky compared to removing the fascia from the prostate.
The surgeon makes an incision in the lateral prostatic fascia, opting for a less aggressive approach to nerve sparing due to the patient's lack of sexual activity.

The surgeon works on defining the lateral edge of the prostate by dissecting around a vein, aiming to visualize the bottom edge of the prostate where it starts to turn and meet the perirectal fat. Despite some bleeding, the surgeon identifies the prostate's bottom edge to assist in accurately placing a clip on the pedicle.

The surgeon uses robotic clip appliers to place clips on the Denonvias tissue on the left side, thinning out the pedicle for easier clipping. The clips are precisely positioned to achieve optimal angles, enhancing control over the procedure.

The surgeon places clips primarily at the pedicle, avoiding distal placement near the apex, and focuses on the neurovascular bundle. The posterolateral edge of the prostate is clearly visible due to the lateral dissection of the prostatic fascia. An assistant port is positioned laterally, using a twelve millimeter air seal port.

The surgeon carefully dissects the pedicle close to the prostate, ensuring nerve sparing by avoiding clipping the nerves before they turn along the posterolateral aspect of the prostate. The edge of the prostate is visible, and the pedicle is taken close to avoid leaving a large wad of tissue that could inadvertently include nerves.

The surgeon incises the Denonvilliers' fascia on the prostate to facilitate nerve sparing, leaving a square of fascia on the posterior side of the prostate. The anterior base tumor is located in the anterior transition zone, allowing for effective nerve sparing without significant risk.
The surgeon makes an incision on the medial side, followed by blunt dissection on the lateral aspect near the neurovascular bundle. The neurovascular bundle is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully navigates around visible veins near the prostate capsule, aiming to avoid previous mistakes. The prostate's edge is visible as it begins to turn, with perirectal fat appearing at the junction. The surgeon identifies the point to penetrate for the pedicle on this side.

The surgeon carefully teases the neurovascular bundle off the prostate, maintaining close proximity to the prostate tissue. The dissection plane on one side appears less defined, possibly due to previous biopsies, resulting in increased tissue stickiness. The surgeon uses a fenestrated Maryland bipolar instrument, noted for its sharp tip, to aid in the dissection.

The surgeon performs precise cautery around the vessels in the renal bed during a partial nephrectomy, using a sharp-tipped instrument to target individual vessels effectively. The nerve-sparing technique is completed despite tissue stickiness. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) is cut cold, resulting in minor bleeding, which is managed without significant blood loss.

The surgeon cuts closer to the prostate to avoid cutting the stitch out, allowing for preservation of urethral length while leaving more tissue on the prostate. The dissection proceeds wider on the dorsal venous complex (DVC) than would be possible with pre-placed stitches or staple techniques, facilitating better control over bleeding with the use of air seal technology.

The surgeon cuts the dorsal venous complex (DVC) further from the prostate, preserving urethral length. The catheter is reinserted by the bedside team. The surgeon then cuts the posterior urethra before addressing the posterior lip, ensuring no prostate tissue is left behind.

The surgeon ensures the urethral length is preserved while widening the dissection margin to completely resect any posterior lip of the prostate. The nerve-sparing technique is employed, keeping the pedicles tight to the prostate to avoid clipping the neurovascular bundle. Clips are placed distal to the node of Cloquet to prevent backflow of lymphatics from the leg, using bipolar cautery for dissection.

The surgeon performs a posterior reconstruction stitch by threading through the edge behind the urethra, grabbing peritoneum near the seminal vesicles, and posterior bladder tissue. A slider knot is used to bring these structures together, aligning the anastomosis and reducing tension.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix barbed suture for the anastomosis, starting at approximately four o'clock on the clock face. The neurovascular bundles are visible, and the surgeon anchors the stitch by grabbing surrounding tissue. Two stitches are placed on the backside to ensure it is watertight.

The surgeon completes the posterior wall of the anastomosis from four to seven o'clock using a barbed suture technique, ensuring it is watertight to prevent leaks. The surgical field is cleared with suction.

The surgeon places the second stitch deep into the corner, securing the posterior urethra and adjacent tissue to prevent leaks. Venous oozing is visible during this process. The surgeon uses the left hand to stabilize the back wall of the urethra while anchoring the stitch to surrounding tissue, including the endopelvic fascia posterior to the bladder.

The surgeon clips the peritoneum to close the twelve port laterally, preventing potential herniation of the appendix into the surgical site. The procedure concludes with the completion of the prostatectomy.
